---
title: "New Zealand Migration Tracker"
subtitle: "Migration trends tracked with Statistics NZ and MBIE data — built by [Autonomous Econ](https://autonomousecon.substack.com)"
---

## Kiwis are leaving faster than they return

Net NZ citizen migration peaked at +24,572 in the 12 months to October 2020 while the border was closed and departures collapsed. By March 2026 it stood at -36,538. Over the same 12 months, non-NZ citizens (including Australians) recorded a net +60,787, down from the post-reopening peak of +176,489 in October 2023.

```{=html}
{{< include data/kiwi-exodus_main.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM552301</p>

## The post-COVID surge has been dominated by migrants in prime-working age groups

Net migration is dominated by people 30 and under throughout the series. The 30-40s age groups in particular account for a much larger share of net migration than in the pre-COVID baseline, a shift that has persisted even as overall volumes have moderated.

```{=html}
{{< include data/kiwi-exodus_age_net.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM552101</p>

## India now supplies one in four non-NZ arrivals

The composition of net migration has shifted markedly since 2022. India and China now dominate inflows, while the "Other" category has compressed as migration becomes more concentrated.

```{=html}
{{< include data/india-surge_net_area.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM552301</p>

Two trends stand out since the border reopened in 2022. India's share of non-NZ arrivals climbed sharply, overtaking China to become the single largest source country, at times accounting for more than one in four non-NZ arrivals.

```{=html}
{{< include data/india-surge_share.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM552301</p>

## Work visas, not students, powered the surge

Work visa arrivals overtook every other category after the border reopened in 2022, including NZ and Australian citizens, and remained the single largest arrival group through 2023.

```{=html}
{{< include data/visa-shift_overall.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM552201</p>

Approved work visa decisions peaked sharply in late 2022 as the Accredited Employer Work Visa (AEWV) replaced Essential Skills. Skilled Work approvals have since moderated following the April 2024 reforms, while the Recognised Seasonal Employer (RSE) scheme, Working Holiday, and Relationship visas have maintained steadier trends.

```{=html}
{{< include data/visa-shift_w1_decisions.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: MBIE Migration Data Explorer</p>

For arrivals whose last permanent residence was India, Work visas account for the overwhelming majority of post-2022 volumes, with Residence and Student visas together remaining a relatively small share.

```{=html}
{{< include data/visa-shift_india_clpr.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM553001</p>

China arrivals tell a different story: Student visas make up a consistently larger share than for India.

```{=html}
{{< include data/visa-shift_china_clpr.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ ITM553001</p>

## Lower-skill work visa share reaches 49% for some nationalities

India's work visa intake has shifted from high-skill toward lower-skill roles since the AEWV launched in mid-2022.

```{=html}
{{< include data/india-surge_skill_shift.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: MBIE Migration Data Explorer</p>
<p class="data-source">Note: W3 covers records where Immigration New Zealand (INZ) captured an occupation (~60% of approved work visa decisions). Open work right holders (Working Holiday, Relationship) are largely excluded. Roles with no recorded skill level are excluded from proportions.</p>

India's lower-skill share (36%) is above countries like the UK (16%) and China (11%), but Vietnam (49%) and Fiji (48%) rank higher. The pattern is not India-specific.

```{=html}
{{< include data/india-surge_country_skill.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: MBIE Migration Data Explorer</p>
<p class="data-source">Note: W3 covers records where INZ captured an occupation (~60% of approved work visa decisions). Open work right holders (Working Holiday, Relationship) are largely excluded. Roles with no recorded skill level are excluded from proportions.</p>

## South Island TAs lead on a per capita basis

International migration is concentrated in a handful of Territorial Authorities. Auckland and Hamilton lead by absolute numbers, but South Island TAs, Queenstown-Lakes in particular, rank far higher on a per-capita basis.

```{=html}
{{< include data/where-they-land_map.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ, Subnational population estimates at 30 June 2025</p>
<p class="data-source">Note: Population estimates are provisional.</p>

```{=html}
{{< include data/where-they-land_top_inflow.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ, Subnational population estimates at 30 June 2025</p>
<p class="data-source">Note: Population estimates are provisional.</p>

Within Auckland, Ōtara-Papatoetoe recorded 102 net migrants per 1,000 residents over the three years ended June 2025, nearly eight times the rate of Rodney (1.2 per 1,000) at the other end of the scale.

```{=html}
{{< include data/where-they-land_auckland_map.html >}}
```

<p class="data-source">Source: Statistics NZ, Subnational population estimates at 30 June 2025</p>
<p class="data-source">Note: Population estimates are provisional. Map boundaries: Stats NZ Territorial Authority Local Board 2025 (Clipped).</p>

<p class="data-source">
Built by <a href="https://autonomousecon.substack.com">Autonomous Econ</a> |
<a href="https://github.com/martingeew/nz_migration_streamlit">Source code</a>
</p>
